method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The island’s present name honours Jacques Marie Boudin de Tromelin de La Nuguy, captain of the French corvette ''Dauphine''.[1] Tromelin reached the island on 29 November 1776 and rescued eight enslaved Malagasy people who had been stranded there for 15 years.[1] The island was formerly known as the Isle of Sand; its French name is ''Île Tromelin''.[1]

==Description==
Tromelin Island is a low-lying, flat coral island of about 1 square kilometre in the Indian Ocean, roughly 450 kilometres east of Madagascar and 500 kilometres north of Réunion.[4] It is often described as a desert island, with a sparse vegetation cover of low bushes and no trees.[5] Aeolian sedimentation has contributed to the island’s topography, and strong winds on Tromelin are concentrated in a narrow directional window between 100° and 140°.[3]

The island is administered by France as part of the Scattered Islands district of the French Southern and Antarctic Lands, and it is uninhabited except for a meteorological station that has operated since 1949.[4] The station monitors cyclones and supports regional weather forecasting, while the island also functions as a sanctuary for nesting sea turtles.[4][5] Tromelin has supported seabird colonies, including masked boobies and red-footed boobies, but introduced rats severely reduced its native bird and plant life before eradication efforts were carried out in 2005.[1][2] Following the removal of rodents, native plants began to recover and several bird species returned, making the island an example of ecological restoration on small Indian Ocean islands.[1][2]

==Fauna and flora==
Green sea turtles are the principal non-avian vertebrates recorded as nesting on Tromelin, while native terrestrial mammals, reptiles and amphibians are absent.[1] Seabirds dominate the island’s fauna, and historical evidence indicates that six to eight species once bred there in large numbers before predation by introduced Norway rats sharply reduced the community.[1][2] By 2005, only two booby species were still breeding, and their reproductive success was limited.[1] Following rat eradication, the breeding seabird assemblage expanded naturally to seven species by 2022, while the number of breeding pairs rose from 353 to 4,758.[1][4] The recovery has been attributed mainly to improved nesting success and recolonisation after predator removal, rather than to human-assisted reintroduction.[1][4]

Non-breeding seabirds also use the island, with eleven species recorded between 2017 and 2021, including terns, frigatebirds, tropicbirds, brown skua, brown booby and Mascarene petrel.[3] Great and lesser frigatebirds were observed using Tromelin regularly as a roost, while white-tailed and red-tailed tropicbirds occasionally landed or displayed, suggesting possible future breeding attempts.[3] Mice are reported as the only introduced mammals remaining on the island after rat eradication.[3]

The native flora is a small assemblage of salt-tolerant littoral plants adapted to wind, salt spray and limited freshwater.[1] Before major invasive-species impacts, the vegetation was dominated by the shrub Tournefortia argentea, also known as tree heliotrope, together with fewer than ten herbaceous taxa.[1] Rat browsing and seed predation reduced native plant cover substantially before the 2005–2006 eradication programme.[1][2] Monitoring after eradication recorded a recovery of native vegetation, with seven native plant taxa present by 2013 and increased density and height of Tournefortia argentea.[1][4] A few introduced plants, including coconut palms, persist but do not dominate the island’s vegetation.[1] Conservation management focuses on preventing further introductions of invasive species that could reverse the recovery of both vegetation and seabird habitat.[1][4]

==History==
The island entered French nautical records in the 1720s, when the French navigator Jean Marie Briand de la Feuillée recorded it as Île de Sable, or “Isle of Sand”.[1] Its present name commemorates Chevalier de Tromelin, commander of the Dauphine, whose arrival at the island on 29 November 1776 is also cited as the basis of the French historical claim to sovereignty.[1][3]

In July 1761, the French ship Utile, carrying an illicit cargo of enslaved people, was wrecked near the island; about twenty sailors and seventy or more enslaved people drowned, with many of the latter trapped below deck because the hatches had been locked or nailed down.[1][2] A group of castaways survived on the island for about fifteen years before the Dauphine reached them in 1776.[2][3] Chevalier de Tromelin rescued eight survivors—seven women and an infant—and took them to Mauritius, where they were later freed.[3] The episode became central to the island’s later historical memory, and has been the subject of archaeological research, reports by Max Guérout, and the documentary Tromelin, the Island of Forgotten Slaves.[5]

Later accounts recorded physical traces of the castaways’ occupation, including reports by British sailors who saw remains of “stone” houses and neatly arranged graves.[2] A UNESCO-sponsored French archaeological mission, involving archaeologists and divers, investigated both the wreck and the island’s occupation sites.[2] The team uncovered dwelling walls built from coral blocks and compacted sand, as well as a large oven containing remains of turtles, birds and shellfish.[2]

The island’s sovereignty has remained disputed: France bases its claim on the 1776 arrival of the Dauphine, while Mauritius argues that Tromelin should have passed to Britain under the Treaty of Paris of 1814 rather than continuing to be administered as a dependency of Réunion.[1] The United Nations has not recognized Mauritian sovereignty over Tromelin.[1]

==Climate==
Tromelin Island has a tropical savanna climate (Köppen climate classification Aw), with warm conditions throughout the year and little seasonal temperature variation.[2] Rainfall is strongly seasonal, with a wetter period from November to April associated with the intertropical convergence zone and drier conditions from May to October.[2] Mean annual precipitation is about 1,074 mm, with the highest monthly totals generally occurring during the austral summer, especially around February.[2] The climate is also shaped by prevailing south-easterly trade winds, which are often strong and contribute to high evaporation and persistent wind exposure.[2] The island’s position in the south-west Indian Ocean makes it useful for monitoring tropical cyclones affecting the wider region, particularly systems moving toward Madagascar; a cyclone destroyed the island’s weather station in the 1950s before it was rebuilt.[4]
